questions.questionText: Reed-Sternberg cells (large binucleated 'owl-eye' cells) are pathognomonic of:
questions.explanation: Reed-Sternberg (RS) cells: large binucleated or multinucleated cells with prominent nucleoli ('owl-eye' appearance). CD15+, CD30+, CD45−. Necessary but not sufficient for diagnosis of Hodgkin lymphoma (also see inflammatory background). Variants: lacunar RS (nodular sclerosis), mummified RS, LP cells/popcorn cells (nodular lymphocyte-predominant HL — CD20+, CD45+). Most common type: nodular sclerosis. Best prognosis: lymphocyte-rich. Worst: lymphocyte-depleted. Reference: Robbins — Hodgkin Lymphoma.

questions.optionB: AML (especially APL/M3 — most abundantly as faggot cells)
questions.explanation: Auer rods: crystallized azurophilic granules (contain myeloperoxidase) — needle-like, pink/red cytoplasmic inclusions. PATHOGNOMONIC for AML. Most prominently seen in APL (AML-M3, t(15;17) PML-RARα) as 'faggot cells' (bundles of Auer rods). APL: high DIC risk, treated with ATRA (all-trans retinoic acid) + arsenic trioxide. AML markers: myeloperoxidase+, CD13+, CD33+. ALL: PAS+ (TdT+, CD10+). Reference: Robbins — AML.

questions.optionB: Neoplastic plasma cells in bone marrow, M-protein (monoclonal Ig), lytic bone lesions, and Bence-Jones proteinuria
questions.explanation: Multiple myeloma: malignant plasma cell neoplasm in bone marrow. Features (CRAB): Calcium elevated, Renal insufficiency, Anemia, Bone lytic lesions (punched-out, NO blastic response). M-protein spike on SPEP (IgG most common). Bence-Jones protein: free light chains in urine (κ or λ). Rouleaux formation on blood smear. Amyloidosis (AL type). Russell bodies (intracytoplasmic Ig). Bone marrow: >10% clonal plasma cells. Reference: Robbins — Multiple Myeloma.

questions.questionText: Chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) is characterized by:
questions.optionB: Accumulation of mature-appearing small lymphocytes (B cells: CD5+, CD19+, CD20+, CD23+), smudge cells
questions.explanation: CLL: most common leukemia in adults (>60 years) in Western countries. Small, mature-appearing B lymphocytes accumulate. Smudge cells (basket cells) on peripheral smear — fragile lymphocytes. Immunophenotype: CD5+ (usually a T cell marker), CD19+, CD20 (dim+), CD23+. Warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia (Coombs+). Richter transformation: aggressive large B-cell lymphoma (poor prognosis). Staging: Rai (0-IV) or Binet (A-C). Reference: Robbins — CLL.

questions.questionText: Hereditary spherocytosis is caused by defects in:
questions.optionB: RBC membrane/cytoskeleton proteins (spectrin, ankyrin, band 3, band 4.2)
questions.explanation: Hereditary spherocytosis: most common inherited hemolytic anemia in Northern Europeans. AD (most common). Defective RBC membrane cytoskeleton (spectrin, ankyrin most common) → loss of membrane surface → spherocytes (small, dense, no central pallor). Features: anemia, splenomegaly, jaundice, gallstones (pigment). Diagnosis: osmotic fragility test (increased), eosin-5-maleimide (EMA) binding test, spherocytes on smear. Treatment: splenectomy (curative). Reference: Robbins — Hereditary Spherocytosis.

questions.optionB: t(8;14) c-MYC translocation, EBV (endemic form), 'starry sky' pattern
questions.explanation: Burkitt lymphoma: highly aggressive B-cell NHL, fastest-growing human tumor (nearly 100% Ki-67). t(8;14): c-MYC (chromosome 8) juxtaposed to IgH (chromosome 14) → MYC overexpression. Histology: 'starry sky' pattern (macrophages clearing apoptotic debris). Endemic (African): jaw/facial bones, EBV associated (~100%). Sporadic: abdominal masses (ileocecal). HIV-associated. Chemo-sensitive (CHOP-like regimens). Reference: Robbins — Burkitt.

questions.questionText: Iron deficiency anemia shows all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency anemia: most common anemia worldwide. Labs: LOW serum iron, LOW ferritin (best measure of iron stores), HIGH TIBC (liver makes more transferrin to capture more iron), LOW transferrin saturation (<20%). MCV: low (microcytic). MCH/MCHC: low (hypochromic). RDW: elevated (anisocytosis). Smear: microcytes, target cells, pencil cells. TIBC is INCREASED (not decreased). Causes: chronic blood loss (GI in men/post-menopausal, menstrual in premenopausal women), poor intake, malabsorption. Reference: Robbins — IDA.

questions.questionText: Thalassemia major (β-thalassemia major/Cooley's anemia) presents with:
questions.optionB: Severe anemia, hepatosplenomegaly, skeletal deformities (chipmunk facies, crew-cut skull), and hemosiderin deposition
questions.explanation: β-thalassemia major: absent or markedly reduced β-globin synthesis → excess α chains → precipitate in RBC precursors → ineffective erythropoiesis + hemolysis. Features: severe transfusion-dependent anemia (presents 6 months when HbF switches to HbA), massive hepatosplenomegaly (extramedullary hematopoiesis), skeletal changes (marrow expansion — chipmunk facies, crew-cut appearance on skull X-ray), iron overload (transfusion + increased absorption). Target cells on smear. Reference: Robbins — Thalassemia.

questions.questionText: TTP (thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura) is caused by deficiency of:
questions.explanation: TTP: deficiency of ADAMTS13 (acquired autoantibodies or inherited) → ultra-large vWF multimers persist → spontaneous platelet aggregation → microthrombi → thrombocytopenia + MAHA (schistocytes). Classic pentad: thrombocytopenia, microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (schistocytes), fever, renal dysfunction, neurological symptoms. Treatment: plasma exchange (URGENT — provide ADAMTS13, remove antibodies). Do NOT give platelets. HUS: similar but Shiga toxin-mediated (E. coli O157:H7), primarily renal. Reference: Robbins — TTP.

questions.questionText: Hemophilia A is caused by deficiency of:
questions.explanation: Hemophilia A: X-linked recessive, Factor VIII deficiency (~80% of hemophilia cases). Features: hemarthrosis (most common — knees), deep tissue bleeding, hematomas, prolonged bleeding after surgery/trauma. Labs: prolonged aPTT, normal PT, normal bleeding time, normal platelet count. Mixing study corrects aPTT (no inhibitor). Hemophilia B (Christmas disease): Factor IX deficiency (clinically identical). Treatment: factor VIII replacement (recombinant), DDAVP (mild cases), emicizumab (bispecific antibody). Reference: Robbins — Hemophilia.

questions.questionText: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia (warm type) is mediated by:
questions.optionB: IgG antibodies (react at 37°C, positive direct Coombs test)
questions.explanation: Warm AIHA: IgG autoantibodies bind RBCs at body temperature (37°C) → splenic macrophage Fc receptor-mediated phagocytosis (extravascular hemolysis) → spherocytes. Direct Coombs (DAT): positive (anti-IgG on RBC surface). Causes: idiopathic (50%), SLE, CLL, drugs (methyldopa, penicillin). Treatment: corticosteroids, splenectomy, rituximab. Cold AIHA: IgM (reacts <30°C) → complement fixation → agglutination (Mycoplasma, EBV). Reference: Robbins — AIHA.

questions.optionB: JAK2 V617F mutation, increased RBC mass, low EPO, and risk of thrombosis
questions.explanation: Polycythemia vera (PV): myeloproliferative neoplasm with JAK2 V617F mutation (~95%). Increased RBC mass (Hb/Hct elevated), often with leukocytosis and thrombocytosis. Low serum EPO (autonomous RBC production). Features: facial plethora, splenomegaly, pruritus (especially after bathing), thrombosis (major cause of morbidity/mortality), aquagenic pruritus. Treatment: phlebotomy, hydroxyurea, low-dose aspirin. Risk of transformation to AML or myelofibrosis. Reference: Robbins — PV.
